Glyph strings are dash-joined, e.g. za-ke-ro. Probes are limited; OPEN ends the episode.

4. From Amber Sump we went east into Loamgate.
5. From Loamgate we went east into Haldenook.

So the number of distinct chambers named in the log is 6.

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)

ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape

(in gilds) and needs footage (in spans). Grant a subset of stalls so
the total fee is as large as possible while every rule holds.

S3 Mossgable (herb): fee 11, footage 7
S4 Dovetallow (cloth): fee 38, footage 5
S5 Quaverley (cloth): fee 54, footage 8
S6 Nettlecombe (herb): fee 11, footage 5
S7 Thistledown (cloth): fee 10, footage 8
S8 Rushlight (herb): fee 12, footage 8

Rules:
- Total footage of granted stalls must not exceed 39 spans.

The job: pick stalls so the fees add up as high as possible, respecting a footage cap of 39 spans.

First, fee per span, so I know who earns their footage.
S4 leads at 38/5 = 7.6 gilds a span; then S1 at 6.8, S5 at 6.8, S2 at 6.2, S6 at 2.2.
At the bottom, S7 earns only 1.2 a span.

Now walk that order greedily, taking whatever still fits.
Take S4 (fee 38, 5 spans) — footage now 5.
Take S1 (fee 41, 6 spans) — footage now 11.
Take S5 (fee 54, 8 spans) — footage now 19.
Take S2 (fee 31, 5 spans) — footage now 24.
Take S6 (fee 11, 5 spans) — footage now 29.
Take S3 (fee 11, 7 spans) — footage now 36.
S8 fails here: footage would reach 44, over the cap of 39.
S7 fails here: footage would reach 44, over the cap of 39.
So the greedy pass holds S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, and S6: fee 186, footage 36 of 39.

Greedy lands at 186 gilds. Can I beat it?
The leftover fees nag at me: S8 (fee 12). Try a trade.
Dropping S6 frees 5 spans and gives up 11 gilds.
Bringing in S8 needs 8 spans and pays 12 gilds.
Footage: 36 - 5 + 8 = 39, within the cap of 39.
Fee: 186 - 11 + 12 = 187, up 1. I take that.

Any more juice in the leftovers?
Could S6 (fee 11) come in on top? No: footage would reach 44, over the cap of 39.
Swap S3 (fee 11) out for S7 (fee 10)? That just loses 1 gilds. No.

Last check on S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, and S8: footage 6 + 5 + 7 + 5 + 8 + 8 = 39, within 39.

I settle there: grant S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, and S8, worth 187 gilds in total.

6. From Palegrotto we went down into Grubline.

Four rune wards seal a vault door: mott, osk, quen, drell.
Each ward holds one whole-number charge from 1 to 9. Charges may
repeat unless a binding forbids it. The warding script declares:

1. The quen and drell charges add up to exactly 11.
2. Together, osk and quen hold exactly 9.

4. The mott and drell charges are not equal.

6. Together, mott and quen hold exactly 16.
7. The mott charge is exactly 7 more than the osk charge.

Either the bindings force a single possible assignment of charges,
or they contradict one another and cannot all hold at once.

ward. If they contradict, give exactly the word IMPOSSIBLE.

2. $O + Q = 9$

5. $O + D = 4$
6. $M + Q = 16$
7. $M = O + 7$

From constraint 5: $O + D = 4$.
Since charges are integers from 1 to 9, possible pairs for $(O, D)$ are $(1, 3), (2, 2), (3, 1)$.
So $O$ can be 1, 2, or 3.
And $D$ can be 3, 2, or 1.

From constraint 7: $M = O + 7$.
If $O = 1$, $M = 8$.
If $O = 2$, $M = 9$.
If $O = 3$, $M = 10$.
However, the charge must be between 1 and 9. So $M$ cannot be 10.
This eliminates the case $O = 3$.
So we are left with two possibilities for $(O, M)$: $(1, 8)$ or $(2, 9)$.
Correspondingly, from constraint 5, $D$ is 3 or 1.
So we have two scenarios:
Scenario A: $O=1, M=8, D=3$.
Scenario B: $O=2, M=9, D=1$.

Now let's check the other constraints with these scenarios.
Constraint 2: $O + Q = 9$.
In Scenario A ($O=1$): $1 + Q = 9 \Rightarrow Q = 8$.
In Scenario B ($O=2$): $2 + Q = 9 \Rightarrow Q = 7$.

Constraint 1: $Q + D = 11$.
In Scenario A ($Q=8, D=3$): $8 + 3 = 11$. This holds.
In Scenario B ($Q=7, D=1$): $7 + 1 = 8 \neq 11$. This fails.
So Scenario B is invalid.

Let's verify Scenario A against all constraints.
Scenario A values: $M=8, O=1, Q=8, D=3$.
1. $Q + D = 8 + 3 = 11$. (Satisfied)
2. $O + Q = 1 + 8 = 9$. (Satisfied)

4. $M \neq D \Rightarrow 8 \neq 3$. (Satisfied)
5. $O + D = 1 + 3 = 4$. (Satisfied)
6. $M + Q = 8 + 8 = 16$. (Satisfied)
7. $M = O + 7 \Rightarrow 8 = 1 + 7$. (Satisfied)

The charges are:
Mott: 8
Osk: 1
Quen: 8
Drell: 3

The question asks for the charge of the osk ward.
$O = 1$.

Let's double check if I missed anything.
"Each ward holds one whole-number charge from 1 to 9."
My values are 8, 1, 8, 3. All are in [1, 9].
"Charges may repeat unless a binding forbids it."
$Q=8, M=8$. No binding forbids $Q=M$.
$M \neq D$ is a binding, satisfied.
$Q \neq D$ is a binding, satisfied.

The logic seems solid.
Let's re-read carefully to ensure no misinterpretation.
"Either the bindings force a single possible assignment ... or they contradict".
I found a single assignment.

Let's quickly check the contradiction path again just to be sure.
$O+D=4$ and $O+Q=9$ and $Q+D=11$.
Summing the first two: $O+D+O+Q = 4+9 = 13 \Rightarrow 2O + D + Q = 13$.
Substitute $Q+D=11$: $2O + (11 - O) = 13 \Rightarrow O + 11 = 13 \Rightarrow O = 2$.

$O + Q = 9$
$Q + D = 11$

From $O+D=4$, $D = 4-O$.
From $O+Q=9$, $Q = 9-O$.
Substitute into $Q+D=11$:
$(9-O) + (4-O) = 11$
$13 - 2O = 11$
$2O = 2$
$O = 1$.

Okay, my manual scenario check earlier yielded $O=1$ for Scenario A and $O=2$ for Scenario B.
Let's re-verify Scenario B calculation.
Scenario B was derived from $O=2$.
If $O=2$, then $D=2$ (since $O+D=4$).
If $O=2$, then $Q=7$ (since $O+Q=9$).
Check $Q+D=11$: $7+2=9 \neq 11$.

$Q \neq D \Rightarrow 8 \neq 3$. True.

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:

SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

(the planted bug):

1: SUB B 7
2: SUB C 5
3: ADD B 2
4: COPY C D
5: IFZ A
6: ZERO A
7: SWAP C B
8: SUB D 8

original routine yields, 'buggy' is what the routine above yields.

T1: start A=5 B=3 C=2 D=0 | correct A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=2 D=0 -> FAIL

T3: start A=9 B=1 C=0 D=0 | correct A=0 B=0 C=0 D=0 | buggy A=0 B=0 C=2 D=0 -> FAIL

SPOOL is a tiny register language. Four registers A, B, C, D hold
non-negative integers. One instruction per line, executed top to bottom:
ADD r n = add n to register r
SUB r n = subtract n from r (floor at 0)
COPY r s = set r to the current value of s
SWAP r s = exchange the values of r and s
ZERO r = set r to 0
IFZ r = if r is 0, skip the next line

Below is the CORRECT copy of a loom-house routine, exactly as it should run.

1: SUB D 2
2: COPY C D
3: SWAP C B
4: SWAP A C

Start registers: A=0 B=1 C=8 D=0.
After the routine finishes, what value does register A hold?

End your reply with exactly one final line of the form:
ANSWER: <value>

think:
I need to run this routine exactly as printed and report what register A holds when it finishes.
The registers start at A=0 B=1 C=8 D=0.

Scanning first: only line 4 touches A; I'll still trace the whole routine in order to keep every register straight.

Line 1 is SUB D 2: 0 minus 2 would go negative, so D floors at 0. Now A=0 B=1 C=8 D=0.
Line 2 is COPY C D: C takes D's value, so C=0. Now A=0 B=1 C=0 D=0.
Line 3 is SWAP C B: now C=1 and B=0. Now A=0 B=0 C=1 D=0.
Line 4 is SWAP A C: now A=1 and C=0. Now A=1 B=0 C=0 D=0.
That was the last line, so the routine ends with A=1 B=0 C=0 D=0.

Before I commit, let me double-check A's history.
A begins at 0, then line 4 (SWAP A C) makes it 1, and it never changes again. That matches my trace.
So at the end of the routine, A holds 1.

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A

A patchwheel is an ordered list of rewrite rules run on a token tape:
rule 1 first on the whole tape, each later rule on the result. Forms:
every A becomes B = every token A turns into B
swap first last = exchange the first and last tokens
reverse all = reverse the tape
rotate left = the first token moves to the end
rotate right = the last token moves to the front
drop after A = left to right, delete the token right after each A
  (a deleted token is not scanned)
drop before A = left to right, delete the kept token right before
  each A (an A can delete an earlier A)
double A = every A turns into A A
